what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard item that distinguishes two kinds of annotation gestures on markup surfaces — inline marks (in-text edits/replacements) and margin marks (commentary anchored to spans) — and argues they require distinct anchors, payloads, envelope shapes, and UI renderings.
observations: Claims every markup surface has two semantic zones: inline marks (surgical, at character/word boundaries, proposing insert/delete/replace) and margin marks (beside text, commentary about a span or subtree).
States that Tim's word highlight is an inline mark, while his comment on a whole section is a margin mark on a subtree.
Asserts that the block-hierarchy gesture (BD-B) is built only for the margin case and lacks an inline-edit primitive.
Sketches a phone inline-edit flow: native selection of a word, then 'replace with' typing, producing an envelope carrying old text plus new text as a proposed diff.
Proposes a rendering convention: margin marks in a thin gutter as small typed icons aligned to anchor lines; inline marks rendered as struck-through old text plus distinct-color new text.
References external items/threads by shorthand: BD-B, BD-D ADDITIONS, MSG2.
standouts: Strong first-principles claim that inline and margin marks are different physical/semantic registers and should not produce the same envelope shape.
Concrete mobile UI proposal: a narrow left-edge rail of icon dots for margin marks, making the document itself the index of marks.
Identification of a missing primitive: inline word replacement/diff preview is not supported by the current block-hierarchy gesture.
conventions: Uses architectural vocabulary: registers, envelope shape, anchor point, span, subtree, primitive, payload.
References external design items with project-specific shorthand (BD-B, BD-D ADDITIONS, MSG2).
Written as a design argument with bolded terms and bullet structure.
Assumes shared context about a user/reviewer named Tim and a block-hierarchy system.
concerns: Relies on undefined external references (BD-B, BD-D, MSG2) without explaining them in this file.
Presents implementation and rendering choices as natural consequences of the distinction without supporting evidence in the file.
Contains no code, schema, or examples; it is a pure design assertion.
notes: A design note arguing that markup annotations must separate inline text edits from margin-span commentary and sketching a mobile gutter rendering pattern for margin marks.
questions: What exactly are BD-B and BD-D ADDITIONS in this project?
Who or what is Tim, and what is MSG2?
Is there an existing data model for envelope shape that this note is critiquing?
How would the proposed inline diff envelope integrate with or replace the block-hierarchy primitive?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to articulate the margin-vs-inline distinction and its UI implications; the file does exactly that, so it matches.
apparent_themes: semantic distinction between content-level and meta-level annotations
intention_signals: It appears to be trying to establish a first-principles taxonomy of annotation gestures so the system can model inline edits and margin comments with distinct data structures, payloads, and renderings.
novelty: The explicit framing of inline and margin marks as two physical/semantic registers requiring different envelope shapes, and the concrete phone UI of a left-edge icon rail that makes the document itself an index of margin marks.
contribution: A clear conceptual split between inline replacement marks and margin commentary marks, plus a concrete mobile rendering convention (gutter icon rail for margin marks, inline struck-through/colored diff for inline marks) that a fused system could keep when designing annotation envelopes and UI.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note arguing that markup surfaces need separate inline-edit and margin-comment registers with distinct anchors, payloads, and rendering, and proposing a mobile gutter-icon UI for margin marks and inline diff styling for inline marks.
intention_for_embedding: To record a design rationale for distinguishing inline and margin annotation gestures and to propose corresponding envelope shapes and mobile rendering patterns.
